translation: With the king killed, the citizens celebrated their freedom.
options: ablative absolute
participial phrase
temporal clause
purpose clause
explanation: rēge occīsō is an ablative absolute: rēge (ablative noun) + occīsō (PPP of occīdō, ablative). The key signal is that the king is NOT the subject of the main clause (cīvēs are). An ablative absolute is grammatically independent — both noun and participle are ablative and separate from the main sentence.

translation: With the leader having set out, the soldiers were afraid.
options: ablative absolute
temporal clause
participial phrase
indirect statement
explanation: duce profectō: duce (ablative of dux) + profectō (PPP/perfect participle of the deponent proficīscor, ablative). The leader is not the subject of the main clause (mīlitēs are). Both parts are in the ablative and grammatically independent — the hallmark of the ablative absolute.

sentence: hīs rēbus audītīs, omnēs perterritī sunt.
display: hīs rēbus audītīs, omnēs perterritī sunt.
translation: These things having been heard, everyone was terrified.
options: ablative absolute
participial phrase
purpose clause
temporal clause
explanation: hīs rēbus audītīs: hīs rēbus (ablative plural of hae rēs) + audītīs (PPP of audiō, ablative plural). The things heard are not the subject (omnēs are). This is a typical ablative absolute with a demonstrative pronoun + PPP, giving a before-and-after sequence.

translation: With the soldiers leaving, the enemy attacked the city.
options: ablative absolute
temporal clause
participial phrase
purpose clause
explanation: mīlitibus discēdentibus: mīlitibus (ablative) + discēdentibus (present active participle of discēdō, ablative plural). The soldiers are not the subject of oppugnāvērunt (hostēs are). An ablative absolute can use a present participle (simultaneous action) or PPP (prior action).

translation: With a messenger sent, the general was able to reply.
options: ablative absolute
purpose clause
participial phrase
result clause
explanation: nūntiō missō: nūntiō (ablative of nūntius) + missō (PPP of mittō, ablative). The messenger is not the subject of potuit (imperātor is). The sending of the messenger is the prior action that enabled the general to respond — ablative absolute sets the scene for the main clause.

sentence: ponte dēlētō, hostēs trānsīre nōn poterant.
display: ponte dēlētō, hostēs trānsīre nōn poterant.
options: With the bridge destroyed, the enemy could not cross.
The bridge having destroyed the enemy, they could not cross.
Although the bridge was destroyed, the enemy crossed.
The enemy destroyed the bridge so that they could cross.
correct: With the bridge destroyed, the enemy could not cross.
explanation: ponte dēlētō: ablative noun (ponte, ablative of pōns) + PPP (dēlētō, ablative of dēleō). The bridge is not the subject — hostēs are. The destruction of the bridge is the condition/prior event. Translate as 'with the bridge destroyed' or 'after the bridge was destroyed'.

sentence: omnibus rēbus parātīs, exercitus profectus est.
display: omnibus rēbus parātīs, exercitus profectus est.
options: With all things prepared, the army set out.
The army prepared all things and set out.
Although all things were prepared, the army set out.
The army set out in order to prepare all things.
correct: With all things prepared, the army set out.
explanation: omnibus rēbus parātīs: omnibus rēbus (ablative plural) + parātīs (PPP of parō, ablative plural). The things are not the subject — exercitus is. Note the agreement: all three words (omnibus, rēbus, parātīs) are ablative plural. The preparation is completed before setting out.

sentence: Caesare imperante, Rōmānī multa bella gessērunt.
display: Caesare imperante, Rōmānī multa bella gessērunt.
options: With Caesar in command, the Romans waged many wars.
Caesar commanded the Romans to wage many wars.
While Caesar waged war, the Romans were in command.
The Romans waged war so that Caesar could command.
correct: With Caesar in command, the Romans waged many wars.
explanation: Caesare imperante: Caesare (ablative of Caesar) + imperante (present active participle of imperō, ablative). Caesar is not the subject of gessērunt (Rōmānī are). Present participle in ablative absolute expresses simultaneous action — while Caesar was commanding, the Romans were waging wars.

sentence: signo datō, mīlitēs in hostēs impetum fēcērunt.
display: signo datō, mīlitēs in hostēs impetum fēcērunt.
options: With the signal given, the soldiers charged the enemy.
The soldiers gave the signal and charged the enemy.
Although the signal was given, the soldiers did not charge.
The soldiers gave the signal in order to charge the enemy.
correct: With the signal given, the soldiers charged the enemy.
explanation: signo datō: signo (ablative of signum) + datō (PPP of dō, ablative). The signal is not the subject — mīlitēs are. impetum facere means 'to make an attack/charge'. The giving of the signal (ablative absolute) precedes and triggers the charge.

options: With peace made, the citizens returned home.
The citizens made peace and returned home.
The citizens returned home in order to make peace.
Although peace was made, the citizens returned home.
correct: With peace made, the citizens returned home.
explanation: pāce factā: pāce (ablative of pāx) + factā (PPP of faciō, ablative feminine singular agreeing with pāce). The peace is not the subject — cīvēs are. pācem facere means 'to make peace'. The making of peace (ablative absolute) is the prior event enabling the return home.
